question: Where should @Transactional be placed in a typical Spring Boot application?
options: On the repository interface methods, since they touch the database directly
On the controller methods, so the full HTTP request is transactional
On the service layer methods that coordinate multiple repository calls
On the entity class, so every field access is transactional

question: A method annotated @Transactional throws a checked IOException mid-way through two DB writes. What happens by default?
options: Both writes are rolled back because any exception triggers rollback
Both writes are committed — Spring only rolls back on unchecked exceptions by default
The first write is rolled back but the second is committed
Spring re-tries the method automatically before committing

question: You need an audit log to persist even when the calling transaction rolls back. Which propagation type achieves this?
options: REQUIRED — joins the existing transaction so it shares the same fate
NESTED — creates a savepoint, but rolls back with the outer transaction on full failure
REQUIRES_NEW — suspends the outer transaction and commits the audit log independently
NEVER — runs without any transaction, so rollback can't affect it

question: What is the self-invocation problem with @Transactional?
options: Calling a @Transactional method recursively causes a stack overflow
Calling a @Transactional method from within the same class bypasses Spring's proxy, so the transaction annotation is ignored
Spring disallows two @Transactional methods in the same class
@Transactional only works on beans injected via constructor injection, not field injection

question: What is the benefit of @Transactional(readOnly = true) on a query method?
options: It prevents the method from being called inside an existing write transaction
It locks the database rows so no other transaction can modify them during the read
Hibernate skips dirty-checking and flush operations, improving read performance
It automatically caches the result in the second-level cache
